---
title: "1.1 Backtesting as a Research Loop"
subtitle: "Video lesson slides"
author: "Algorithmic Trading"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    css: ../../../../styles/reveal.css
    slide-number: true
    controls: true
    progress: true
    transition: slide
    transition-speed: fast
    center: false
    auto-stretch: true
    auto-animate: true
    auto-slide: 9000
    auto-slide-stoppable: true
    show-notes: false
    scrollable: true
    embed-resources: false
execute:
  echo: true
---

## The Backtest Contract

::: columns
::: {.column width="54%"}
A backtest is useful only if it answers this question:

**What would this exact executable rule have done with only historical
information available at the time?**
:::

::: {.column width="46%"}
$$
r^{\text{net}}_t = w_{t-1}r_t - c_t
$$
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Start by making the backtest concrete. It is not an equity curve exercise.
It is an execution rehearsal using historical data.
:::

## Details Move Returns

Small choices can change the result:

- market-on-open versus after-open order
- primary exchange close versus consolidated close
- bid, ask, last trade, high, or low
- stock-market close versus futures-market close

::: {.notes}
The point from Chapter 1 is that published strategy descriptions often omit
implementation details. The backtest has to make them explicit.
:::

## Three Gates

| Gate | Question |
|---|---|
| Mechanical validity | Did we avoid impossible information and impossible trades? |
| Statistical evidence | Is the result unlikely under a clear null model? |
| Future relevance | Could a regime shift make the edge stale? |

::: {.notes}
Do not collapse these into one number. A high Sharpe ratio can still fail any
one of the three gates.
:::

## Pitfall: Look-Ahead Bias

The backtest must never use future information for today's decision.

```python
signal = raw_signal.shift(1)
position = signal.clip(-1, 1)
net_return = position * returns - costs
```

::: {.notes}
The shift is the visible guardrail. Same-day high and low prices are common
sources of accidental look-ahead because they are known only after the day ends.
:::

## Pitfall: Data Snooping

Data snooping appears when the researcher keeps adjusting the rule until the
validation set looks good.

Best defenses:

- simple rules
- fixed validation periods
- cross-validation or walk-forward testing
- small live-money tests before scaling

::: {.notes}
The chapter's practical warning is that out-of-sample data stops being
out-of-sample once it drives repeated model changes.
:::

## Pitfall: Execution Mismatch

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
Stocks:

- delistings
- dividends and splits
- borrow availability
- primary exchange prices
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
Other markets:

- futures rolls
- venue-specific FX quotes
- order-book depth
- microstructure response
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
This slide connects backtesting to automated execution. The strategy is not
tradable until the assumptions match the venue and order type.
:::

## Review Question

Before trusting the result, ask:

**Could the same function run live if we replaced historical data with a live
feed?**

::: {.notes}
If the answer is no, the backtest may still be a research sketch, but it is not
yet an execution-ready strategy.
:::
